coredata.dc:title: Ag and N-doped graphene quantum dots co-modified CuBi2O4 submicron rod photocathodes with enhanced photoelectrochemical activity
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Chunyan
Ma, De-Kun
Yu, Wenyuan
Chen, Wei
Huang, Shaoming
coredata.dc:description: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting that can mimic natural photosynthesis to directly harvest and convert solar energy into clean and sustainable hydrogen energy, has been known as a promising avenue for addressing environmental pollution and energy crisis. Spinel-type CuBi2O4 semiconductor as a promising photocathode material for solar water reduction has attracted much attention because of its many unique advantages. However, PEC activity of pristine CuBi2O4 is far from satisfying because of its poor charge carrier transport and transfer efficiency. Herein we report that Ag+ ions and N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) co-modified CuBi2O4 submicron rods (NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs) photocathode can show enhanced PEC activity. Our work demonstrated that one-dimensional structure, Ag+ ions doping, and NGQDs loading benefited the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and improved light harvesting ability, which were collectively contributed to enhanced PEC activity of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode. As a result, the photocurrent density achieved on NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode under AM1.5G simulated sunlight at 0.3 V vs. RHE was 8.8, 2.3, 1.8, and 5.9-times larger than those obtained on CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, NGQDs/CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO microspheres (MSs) ones, respectively. The present study provides a new strategy for designing low-cost and highly efficient photocathodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CuBi2O4 submicron rods
Doping
Graphene quantum dots
Charge carriers
Photoelectrochemical cell

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the NSFZJ for Distinguished Young Scholars ( LR16B010002 ) and NSFC ( 21673160 , 51372173 , and 51420105002 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the fabrication process of NGQDs/Ag-CBO photoelectrode.
FE-SEM images of the as-obtained CBO SMRs (a), Ag-CBO SMRs (b), and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs (c). Panoramic (d) and magnified (e) TEM images and HRTEM image of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs (f).

(a) XRD patterns of the as-obtained CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs. (b) Magnified XRD patterns correspond to (200) crystal plane for the same samples. High-resolution XPS spectra for Ag 3d (c), Cu 2p (d), Bi 4f (e), O 1s (f), C 1s (g), and N1s (h).

(a) Current density-potential characteristics of FTO/CBO SMR photoelectrodes with different amounts of Ag+ ion doping. (b) Current density-potential curves of FTO/Ag-CBO SMRs with different loading amounts of NGQDs. All of measurements were operated in 0.5 M of Na2SO4 solution under chopped AM1.5G simulated sunlight illumination.

Current density comparisons of FTO/CBO SMRs, FTO/Ag-CBO SMRs, FTO/CBO SMRs/NGQDs, and FTO/Ag-CBO SMRs/NGQDs photoelectrodes (a). Current density comparisons of FTO/Ag-CBO SMRs/NGQDs and FTO/Ag-CBO MSs/NGQDs (b). All of measurements were performed under the same conditions.

UV–Vis absorption spectra (a) and PL spectra (b) of CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/CBO SMRs, respectively. Mott-Schottky plots for FTO/CBO SMRs, FTO/Ag-CBO SMRs, and FTO/Ag-CBO/NGQDs electrodes (c). EIS spectra of FTO/CBO SMRs, FTO/Ag-CBO SMRs, and FTO/Ag-CBO/NGQDs photocathodes measured under AM1.5G simulated sunlight illumination and in the dark at 0.2 V vs. RHE (d). In the legends of Fig. 6d, L and D denotes light irradiation and in the dark, respectively.
Curve of time-dependent photocurrent for FTO/Ag-CBO/NGQDs measured in 0.5 M of Na2SO4 at 0.2 V vs. RHE under AM1.5G simulated sunlight illumination.

head.ce:title: Ag and N-doped graphene quantum dots co-modified CuBi2O4 submicron rod photocathodes with enhanced photoelectrochemical activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325027, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Carbon Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting that can mimic natural photosynthesis to directly harvest and convert solar energy into clean and sustainable hydrogen energy, has been known as a promising avenue for addressing environmental pollution and energy crisis. Spinel-type CuBi2O4 semiconductor as a promising photocathode material for solar water reduction has attracted much attention because of its many unique advantages. However, PEC activity of pristine CuBi2O4 is far from satisfying because of its poor charge carrier transport and transfer efficiency. Herein we report that Ag+ ions and N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) co-modified CuBi2O4 submicron rods (NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs) photocathode can show enhanced PEC activity. Our work demonstrated that one-dimensional structure, Ag+ ions doping, and NGQDs loading benefited the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and improved light harvesting ability, which were collectively contributed to enhanced PEC activity of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode. As a result, the photocurrent density achieved on NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode under AM1.5G simulated sunlight at 0.3 V vs. RHE was 8.8, 2.3, 1.8, and 5.9-times larger than those obtained on CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, NGQDs/CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO microspheres (MSs) ones, respectively. The present study provides a new strategy for designing low-cost and highly efficient photocathodes.

Ag+ ions and NGQDs co-modified CuBi2O4 submicron rod photocathodes showed enhanced photoelectrochemical activity because of efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers and improved light harvesting ability.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution and energy crisis from excessive consumption of fossil fuel have become formidable challenge to sustainable development of human society [1–4]. How to find green and renewable energy is urgently needed. Since Honda and Fujishima first reported TiO2 photoelectrodes to produce H2 in 1972 [5], PEC water splitting that can effectively convert solar energy into clean energy‑hydrogen, has been known as a promising avenue for addressing the above concerns [6–8]. In addition, compared with photovoltaics-coupled electrolysis, PEC water splitting has lower cost via cheap, scalable, easily produced materials [9]. However, widely used pristine TiO2 photoelectrode (3.2 eV) only absorb UV-light (<5% in solar spectrum), which leads to a low solar utilization efficiency [10]. Therefore many efforts have been made to develop highly efficient visible-light-responding photoelectrodes. Compared with abundant n-type photoanodes being available, there are only a few promising candidates for efficient p-type photocathodes for water reduction to produce hydrogen [11]. Although several p-type semiconductors such as p-doped Si [12], Cu2O [13], CuRhO2 [14], InP [15], WSe2 [16] and CuIn0.5Ga0.5Se2 [17] have been used for PEC water reduction, high cost, poor photostability in aqueous solution, and complicated fabrication technique of the photoelectrodes limited their application range. In addition, the maximum cathodic photocurrent onset potentials relative to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) achieved on these materials were more negative than 0.6 V [18]. In a tandem cell more positive photocurrent onset for water reduction is helpful to obtain maximum photocurrent density for unbiased water splitting. Therefore it is highly desirable to develop low-cost, stable, efficient and easy-fabricated photocathodes with more positive photocurrent onset.

Spinel-type CuBi2O4 semiconductor as a promising photocathode material for solar water reduction has attracted much attention because of its many unique advantages. Firstly, it consists of earth-abundant Cu, Bi and O elements. These elements are inexpensive and low-toxic. Secondly, it has a band gap of 1.5–1.8 eV. Theoretically, the maximum photocurrent of 19.7–29.0 mA cm−2 under AM1.5 illumination will be obtained if all photons with energies higher than the band gap are completely absorbed and utilized [19]. Thirdly, it possesses an appropriate energy band position. Its conduction band potential is more negative than the reduction potential of H+/H2. Therefore it enables solar H2 production in thermodynamics while its valence band is located at more positive potentials than those of other photocathode semiconductors and can usually obtain a more positive photocurrent onset potential (>1.0 V vs. RHE) [20]. Last but not the least, CuBi2O4 photocathode is easy to fabricate. Up to now, various methods such as electrodeposition [21], drop casting [19], and spray pyrolysis [22] have been successfully used to direct fabricate the photocathode. CuBi2O4 powders could be also used to prepare the film photocathodes through drop coating or doctor-blade method [23,24]. However, in either case, PEC activity of pristine CuBi2O4 is far from satisfying because of its poor charge carrier transport and slow reaction kinetics [22]. Therefore some strategies have been explored to improve PEC activity of CuBi2O4 photoelectrodes, such as nanostructure design [25], selective element doping [20], heterojunction construction [26], and noble metal loading [27]. Although these improvements can efficiently boost the PEC activity of CuBi2O4, the related study is still at the primary stage.

Previously, Berglund and co-authors reported that photocurrent of CuBi2O4 could be enhanced by the introduction of elemental Ag [28]. However, recently, Kang et al. have found that Ag+ ions-doped CuBi2O4 films also showed enhanced PEC activity and photostability [20]. For the former, the incorporation of metallic Ag into CuBi2O4 improved the conductivity and charge transport through the film. As for the latter, Ag+ ions substitutionally replaced Bi3+ ions and increased the hole concentration, which improved hole transport. Nevertheless, how to simultaneously improve the transport and separation of both photogenerated electron and hole has never been reported. On the other hand, although the doping (0.7% Ag metal basis) could obviously reduce the usage of Ag than the incorporation of elemental Ag (8.3% Ag metal basis), expensive Pt still needed to be used as co-catalyst to enhance PEC performance of Ag+ ions doped CuBi2O4 [20]. Therefore it is also necessary to develop new low-cost co-catalysts.

Recently, GQDs have attracted much attention because of their low cytotoxity, ease of synthesis, adjustable composition, unique physical and chemical feature, excellent electron transfer efficiency, and tunable light-harvesting range [29]. At present, they have been acted as photosensitizers [30], superfast tunnel for electron transport and co-catalysts in PEC cells [31,32]. However, these GQDs were mainly applied in photoanodes [33]. On the other hand, N doping can improve the conductivity of GQDs. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are few reports on NGQDs modified photocathodes until now [34].

In this work, NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode was prepared through a facile film transfer technique and simple adsorption treatment process. PEC activity of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs was systematically compared with pristine CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO MSs. The influence of microstructure, Ag+ ions doping, and NGQDs loading on PEC activity of CBO was systematically studied. Our work demonstrated that the synergetic effect between Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs modification could significantly endow CBO SMR photoanode with enhanced PEC activity and good photostability.

Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (≥99.99% metals basis), AgNO3 standard solution (0.05 M), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (99.99% metals basis), sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8), Cu mesh, graphene oxide gel (GO: 1 wt%), dimethylformamide (DMF), NaOH, and HNO3 were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. FTO glass (7 Ω/sq, 1 × 2 cm) was purchased from Wuhan Jinge-Solar Energy Technology Co. Ltd. FTO was cleaned by ultrasound in acetone, deionized water, and ethanol for every 15 min in turn and then dried with nitrogen flow before use. The water used in the experiment process was ultrapure water. All the chemicals were used as received unless otherwise stated.

CBO SMRs were prepared through a modified hydrothermal route [35]. In a typical process, 1.21 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 0.9 g of Cu(NO3)3·3H2O and 0.44 g of NaOH were put into 40 mL of deionized water and stirred for 3 h. Then, the as-formed slurry was transferred into a 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and then heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the resultant powders were collected via centrifugation, washed with alcohol and ultrapure water for three times, and finally dried at 60 °C in the oven.

Uniform CBO MSs were synthesized under hydrothermal conditions. In brief, 2 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved into 25 mL of dilute nitric acid solution (0.5 M) under continuous stir at room temperature. Then a piece of Cu mesh (1 × 4 cm2) and 50 mg of Na2S2O8 were added. After vigorous magnetic stirring for 15 min, 12.5 mL of NaOH solution (4 M) was added into the solution dropwise. After that, the as-formed suspension was stirred for 1 h and transferred into a 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, then closed and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After it was cooled to room temperature, the resultant products were collected through the same method as that of Section 2.2.

NGQDs were synthesized through a modified solvothermal route [36]. In a typical process, 0.2 mL of graphene oxide gel was dispersed in 15 mL of DMF. Subsequently, the solution was ultrasonicated for 30 min (100 W, 100 kHz) and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 4.5 h. After cooled to room temperature naturally, the resultant solution was collected via centrifugation to remove the black precipitates. The final brown solution containing NGQDs was obtained and further used.

The synthesis process of Ag-CBO SMRs is as follows. 30 mg of CBO SMRs were dispersed into 10 mL of absolute ethanol containing different volumes of AgNO3 standard solution (0.05 M) and stirred for 3 h to ensure the adsorption of Ag+ ions. The powders dried at 60 °C further were annealed in N2 atmosphere at 550 °C for 4 h.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer furnished with graphite monochromatized Cu Ka radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) at scanning rate of 8°/min in the 2θ range of 20–60°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer equipped with Al Ka radiation (λ = 1253.6 eV). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were conducted on a Nova NanoSEM 200 at an applied voltage of 10 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, high-resolution electron microscopy (HRTEM) images, and elemental mapping images were performed on a JEOL 2010 microscope at an excitation voltage of 200 kV. UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS) were performed on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–Vis spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere, in which BaSO4 was used as the reference. The corresponding absorption spectra were obtained through the conversion of UV–Vis DRS with the Kubelka-Monk function. PL spectra were measured on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA JobinYvon Inc.) under an excitation wavelength of 780 nm at room temperature.

The main preparation process of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs/FTO photocathode was schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . In a typical procedure, 30 mg of CBO SMRs were dispersed into 10 mL of absolute ethanol containing certain volumes of AgNO3 standard solution (0.05 M) and stirred for 3 h. Then 100 μL of the dispersion was evenly drop-coated onto a primary FTO glass substrate (1 × 2 cm2), followed by drying. The as-prepared film was annealed in N2 atmosphere at 550 °C for 4 h with a ramp rate of 10 °C/min to form Ag-CuBi2O4 film. After that, the film was transferred to another glass plate by conductive carbon tape. The excess Ag-CuBi2O4 SMRs were removed by ultrasonication in water. Subsequently, the photocathode was placed in 10 mL of absolute ethanol containing different volumes of NGQDs and stirred for 3 h to promote the adsorption of NGQDs. Finally, the photocathode was dried at 60 °C and covered with epoxy resin in the surrounding area. The fabrication process of CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, NGQDs/CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/CBO MSs photoelectrode was similar to that of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs except that some steps are absent.

All measurements were operated on a standard three electrode system at room temperature, using a Pt wire as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. An aqueous solution of 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution (pH = 6.6) was used as the electrolyte. Photocurrent-potential plots and photocurrent-time curves under chopped light (Super Solar Simulator, AM1.5G) were measured on a CHI 760D electrochemical workstation and the light intensify was adjusted to 100 mW cm−2. The working electrode with an electrolyte-exposed area of 0.5 cm2 was illuminated from the front side. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The photoelectrochemical stability of the electrode was evaluated by the photocurrent densities at a fixed potential of −0.2 vs. RHE. The Mott-Schottky plots were measured within potential range from 0.9 to 1.4 V vs. RHE at a frequency of 1 kHz in the dark. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out at an applied potential of 0.2 V vs. RHE with an amplitude of 5 mV under illumination and in the dark, respectively. For convenient comparisons, all the electrode potentials vs. SCE were converted to RHE scale, using the Nernst equation: E(vs. RHE) = E(vs. SCE) + 0.242 + 0.059 × pH.

Fig. 2a–c presents panoramic FE-SEM images of the as-synthesized products. As seen from Fig. 2a, CBO SMRs with a diameter of ~300 nm and ~2 μm in length could be obtained on a large scale through the facile hydrothermal route. Ag+ ions doping had not obvious influence on the morphology of CBO SMRs, but the surfaces of the CBO SMRs became rough and the lengths were slightly shortened (Fig. 2b). Similarly, the loading of NGQDs does not change the morphology and dispersity of Ag-CBO SMRs (Fig. 2c), either. Fig. 2d and e represent a typical TEM image of the as-prepared NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs. These luminescent NGQDs with small sizes and certain functional groups (Fig. S1, S2) could be firmly adsorbed on the surfaces of Ag-CBO SMRs due to strong electrostatic interaction between them. This conclusion could be further supported by HRTEM image of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs, elemental mapping, and subsequent XPS analyses. As seen from Fig. 2f, the interplane spacing of 0.296 nm is in agreement with (220) plane of CBO, while the lattice fringe separation of 0.245 nm correspond to the typical lattice constant of graphene [37]. As further seen and identified from typical elemental mapping images of the sample (Fig. S3), the signals of Ag, N and C elements besides Cu, Bi and O ones are widely spread, suggesting that Ag+ ions were uniformly doped into the lattice of CBO and NGQDs were distributed on the surfaces of CBO SMRs.

Fig. 3a shows XRD patterns of the as-obtained CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs. All the characteristic diffraction peaks can be indexed to tetragonal-phase CuBi2O4 (JCPDS No. 48-1886). For NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs sample, XRD diffraction peaks of NGQDs were not detected possibly due to their low content. Ag+ ions doping did not incur other phases or impurities, either. As previous reports, Ag+ ions could substitute for Bi3+ lattice sites in CBO matrix under annealing condition [20]. The ionic radius of Bi3+ ions (0.103 nm) is smaller than that of Ag+ ions (0.115 nm). As a result, the diffraction peaks of Ag-CBO SMRs and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs are shifted to low degree direction, compared with pristine CBO SMRs, as seen from magnified XRD patterns located at 20–22° (Fig. 3b). The above result further confirmed that Ag+ ions were doped into the lattice of CBO SMRs. CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs exhibited similar diffraction peak intensity, showing that the doping of Ag+ ions and loading of NGQDs did not obviously change the crystallinity of CBO SMRs. That is to say, no more defects were produced in CBO SMRs after the co-modification of Ag+ ions and NGQDs. This point is very desirable because photogenerated carriers are easy to be recombined at the defects sites.

To further study surface chemical composition and electronic state of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs, XPS analysis was carried out. As observed from survey spectrum, the products consist of Cu, Bi, Ag, O, N, and C elements (Fig. S4), which is consistent with the results of elemental mapping. The oxidation states of Ag, Cu, and Bi in the sample were further investigated by corresponding high-resolution spectra. As shown in Fig. 3c, the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 located at 368.4 and 374.4 eV are in agreement with those of Ag+ ions in Ag+ doped CuBi2O4 [20]. Although the binding energies of metallic Ag are close to those of Ag+ doped CuBi2O4 [38–40], their full widths at half-maximum (FWHM) are obviously different [41]. The FWHM of Ag peak is ca. 0.83 eV [20], while that of Ag+ doped CuBi2O4 obtained here is >1.3 eV. Therefore the above results further showed that Ag+ ions rather than Ag element were doped into CuBi2O4 SMR crystal lattice. The two peaks located at 953.8 (Cu 2p1/2) and 934.0 eV (Cu 2p3/2) and shake-up satellite peaks (marked with asterisks) correspond to Cu2+ ions in oxides (Fig. 3d) [42–44], indicating that Ag+ ion doping does not change the oxidation state of Cu2+ in the CuBi2O4 structure. However, compared with unmodified CuBi2O4, the Bi 4f peaks in NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs were slightly shifted to higher binding energy (164.0 and 158.7 eV) by ca. 0.2 eV (Fig. 3e). This result suggests that Ag+ ions inhabit the sites of Bi3+ ions in CuBi2O4 crystal lattice, which brings an increase in oxidation state of Bi in order to compensate the charge imbalance [45]. O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) could be fitted into two symmetrical peaks (532.1 and 529.6 eV). This indicates the existence of two different types of O species, which are ascribed to adsorbed oxygen of the surface hydroxyls [46] and the lattice oxygen from metal-O bonds [47], respectively. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3g could be deconvoluted into three peaks. The peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.6 eV could be assigned to CC, CN, and COOH bonds in NGQDs. XPS spectrum of N1s core level electrons (Fig. 3h) further confirmed that N was efficiently doped into GQD with a form of pyridinic-like structure [48]. The above results further showed that NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs had been successfully synthesized.

In order to obtain the optimal PEC activity, the dosages of Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading were screened. The PEC activities of the samples were evaluated by LSV curves in 0.5 M of Na2SO4 solution (pH = 6.6) under chopped simulated solar irradiation (AM1.5G, 100 mW cm−2). As shown in Fig. 4a, the photocurrent density of original CuBi2O4 SMRs is relatively low. With increasing content of Ag+ ions doping (from 0 to 2 × 10−5 mol), the photocurrent densities of Ag-CBO SMRs were obviously boosted at the whole potential range. However, the photocurrent densities of Ag-CuBi2O4 SMRs would fast decrease when more Ag+ ions (over 3× 10−5 mol) were doped into CuBi2O4 SMR matrix. Ag+ ions substitutionally replaced Bi3+ ions, which could increase the hole concentration. The appropriate hole concentration would facilitate the hole transport and thus increase photocurrent, while excessively high hole concentration would increase the probability of photogenerated carriers recombination and thus decrease photocurrent. Considering that the sample doped with 0.4 mL of AgNO3 solution (0.05 M) has the best PEC activity, it was further used to load NGQDs. Fig. 4b shows photocurrent density versus potential curves for Ag-CuBi2O4 SMRs with different content of NGQDs. As seen from Fig. 4b, all of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs samples shows higher photocurrent densities than that of Ag-CuBi2O4 SMRs at the whole potential range, which further confirms that the introduction of NGQDs can indeed increase the PEC activity. It is clear that the maximum photocurrent density was obtained when the loading amount of NGQDs was 0.6 mL. The NGQDs could accelerate electron transport and improve hydrogen evolution reaction kinetics and thus enhance the PEC activity of Ag-CuBi2O4 SMRs. However, overmuch loading NGQDs would possibly shield light and affect the light absorption of Ag-CBO SMRs and/or form new recombination centers of photogenerated carriers.

To clearly show the influence of Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading on PEC activity of CBO SMRs, the photocurrent densities versus potential curves for CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, NGQDs/CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs were further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5a, both Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading can increase PEC activity of CBO SMRs. However, obviously, Ag+ ions and NGQDs co-modified CBO SMR photocathode shows the best PEC activity. To be specific, the photocurrent density obtained on NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs at an applied potential of 0.3 V vs. RHE (71 μA cm−2) is 8.8, 2.3, and 1.8-times larger than those of CBO SMRs (8 μA cm−2), Ag-CBO SMRs (30 μA cm−2), and NGQDs/CBO SMRs (39 μA cm−2), respectively. Therefore the synergistic effect between Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading could be ascribed to enhanced PEC activity of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs. In addition, an onset potential >1.0 V vs. RHE could be achieved on the photocathode, which offers more options when combined with n-type semiconductors to fabricate a tandem cell for unassisted water splitting.

One-dimensional SMR structure is also helpful to improve PEC activity of CBO. In order to confirm this point, CBO MSs were synthesized through a modified hydrothermal route. These uniform CBO MSs (Fig. S5) were fabricated into photoelectrodes with the same method as CBO SMRs used. Likewise, both Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading could increase PEC activity of CBO MSs, while Ag+ ions and NGQDs co-modified CBO MSs showed the best PEC activity (Fig. S6). At the optimal contents of Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading, PEC activity of NGQDs/Ag-CBO MSs was obviously inferior to that of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs, as shown in Fig. 5b. Specifically, the photocurrent density for the latter (71 μA cm−2) was 5.9-times larger than the former (12 μA cm−2). Therefore SMRs possess better structural advantages than MSs including shorter migration distance and facile transport path for photogenerated carriers travelling to the interface, which ensures rapid charge separation and suppresses bulk recombination.

To further clarify the roles of Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading, UV–Vis absorption spectra, PL spectra, Mott-Schottky curves, and EIS of CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/CBO SMRs were measured, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 6a, all of the samples show a strong absorption in the range of 200–800 nm. After Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading, spectral responsive range of CBO SMRs was somewhat extended except for enhanced light absorption. Although the earliest absorption onset located at around 1.46 eV, the bandgap energies calculated from (αhν)2 vs. hν for CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs are about 1.80, 1.76, and 1.78 eV (Fig. S7), respectively. Therefore the band gap of CBO SMRs was slightly narrowed after co-modification of Ag+ ions and NGQDs. PL spectra showed that near band edge emission intensity of CBO SMRs was reduced after Ag+ ions doping and became much weaker after co-modification of Ag+ ions and NGQDs (Fig. 6b). The results indicate that Ag+ ions doping benefits the transport of the hole, while the co-modification of Ag+ ions and NGQDs is more helpful to the separation of photogenerated electron and hole. The doping of Ag+ ions into the crystal lattice of CBO SMRs would increase the concentration of hole, which could be reflected by Mott-Schottky curves for CBO SMRs and Ag-CBO SMRs (Fig. 6c). All the samples show negative slopes in the Mott-Schottky curves, indicating that they exhibited p-type semiconductors character. According to the Mott-Schottky equation:

εε 0
N
where C sc is the capacitance of the space charge region, E denotes the applied potential, E fb is the flat band potential, ε is the dielectric constant of the semiconductor, ε0 is the permittivity of free space, and N is the donor density (here it represents hole concentration), Mott-Schottky curve of CBO SMRs has a larger slope than that of Ag-CBO SMRs, indicating that the latter has higher hole concentration than the former. NGQDs loading did not obviously change the slope of Mott-Schottky curve of Ag-CBO SMRs, suggesting that electronic structure of Ag-CBO SMRs was hardly affected by NGQDs. On the other hand, both Ag+ ions doping and NGQDs loading could increase E fb. By fitting the linear portions of the curves separately, it is determined that the flat potentials of CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs are ~1.18, ~1.26, and ~1.32 V with respect to RHE. As a result, E fb of Ag-CBO SMRs and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs was positively shifted by about 80 and 140 mV, respectively, compared with that of CBO SMRs. Considering that the magnitude of band bending (E b) is equal to the difference between the applied potential (E) and E fb [34], the absolute value of E b will increase with enlarged E fb. Therefore larger band bending formed at the interface between the NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs electrode and electrolyte would promote faster charge separation of generated electrons and holes [49]. This point could be further supported by the results of EIS. As shown in Fig. 6d, both under light irradiation and in the dark, Ag-CBO SMRs exhibits smaller semicircle than pristine CBO SMRs, while NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs show the smallest semicircle among them. The results show that Ag+ ions doping can improve charge transfer of CBO SMRs, while Ag+ ions and NGQDs co-modified CBO SMRs exhibit the smallest charge transfer resistance. All of the above results exactly match with those of photocurrent curves for the samples.

One-dimensional microstructure, Ag+ ions doping, and NGQDs loading not only can significantly enhance PEC activity of CBO but also give its good photostability. As shown in Fig. 7 , NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMR electrodes still maintain good photostability after 1 h of continuous testing. SEM observations and XRD investigation of the photoelectrode after PEC reaction showed that its crystal phase (Fig. S8) and morphology (Fig. S9) had not obvious change, indicating that Ag+ ions and NGQDs co-modified CBO SMRs electrodes possess good PEC stability. It's worth mentioning that no sacrificial agents and additional noble metal co-catalysts were used in the present reaction system.

In summary, CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs have been synthesized and the corresponding materials have been further fabricated into CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathodes through a facile film transfer technique. The co-modification of Ag+ ions and NGQDs could improve light absorption and extend spectral responsive range of CBO SMRs, and facilitate the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers, which were collectively contributed to enhanced PEC activity of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode. The largest photocurrent density was achieved on NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs due to the synergistic effect between Ag+ ions and NGQDs. As a result, the photocurrent density of NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs at an applied potential of 0.3 V vs. RHE is 8.8, 2.3, and 1.8-times larger than those of CBO SMRs, Ag-CBO SMRs, and NGQDs/CBO SMRs, respectively. In addition, onset potential with >1.0 V vs. RHE could be achieved on NGQDs/Ag-CBO SMRs photocathode, which shows promising application in the field of tandem cell for unassisted water splitting. Our work not only demonstrates how to rationally adjust the separation and transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes but also provide a new strategy for designing highly efficient photoelectrodes for solar energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.03.172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phys. Rev. B: Condens. Matter Mater. Phys.
